question:
Problem B-6
Let \( p \) and \( n \) be positive integers. Suppose that the numbers \( c_{h, k}(h=1,2, \ldots, n ; k=1,2, \ldots, p h) \) satisfy \( 0<c_{h, k}<1 \). Prove that
\[
\left(\sum \frac{c_{h, k}}{h}\right)^{2}<2 p \sum c_{h, k}
\]
where each summation is over all admissible ordered pairs \( (h, k) \).
solution:
B-6.

Let \( a_{h}=\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p h} c_{h, k}\right) / h \). Clearly, \( 0 \leqslant a_{h} \leqslant p \). We now prove that \( \left(\sum_{h-1}^{n} a_{h}\right)^{2} \leqslant 2 p \sum_{h-1}^{n}\left(h a_{h}\right) \), which is equivalent to the assertion of the problem, by induction on \( n \).

For \( n=1 \), one has \( a_{1}^{2} \leqslant p a_{1} \leqslant 2 p a_{1} \) as required. Suppose the inequality established for \( n=m \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_{h=1}^{m+1} a_{h}\right)^{2} & =\left(\sum_{h=1}^{m} a_{h}\right)^{2}+2 a_{m+1} \sum_{h=1}^{m} a_{h}+a_{m+1}^{2} \\
& <2 p \sum_{h=1}^{m}\left(h a_{h}\right)+2 a_{m+1} p m+2 p a_{m+1} \\
& <2 p\left[(m+1) a_{m+1}+\sum_{h=1}^{m}\left(h a_{h}\right)\right]=2 p \sum_{h=1}^{m+1}\left(h a_{h}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-6
Let \( multpar \) and \( totcount \) be positive integers. Suppose that the numbers \( coeffhk(indexh=1,2, \ldots, totcount ; indexk=1,2, \ldots, multpar\, indexh) \) satisfy \( 0<coeffhk<1 \). Prove that
\[
\left(\sum \frac{coeffhk}{indexh}\right)^{2}<2\,multpar \sum coeffhk
\]
where each summation is over all admissible ordered pairs \( (indexh, indexk) \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-6.

Let \( rowmean=\left(\sum_{indexk=1}^{multpar\, indexh} coeffhk\right) / indexh \). Clearly, \( 0 \leqslant rowmean \leqslant multpar \). We now prove that
\[
\left(\sum_{indexh=1}^{totcount} rowmean\right)^{2} \leqslant 2\,multpar \sum_{indexh=1}^{totcount}\bigl(indexh\, rowmean\bigr),
\]
which is equivalent to the assertion of the problem, by induction on \( totcount \).

For \( totcount=1 \), one has \( rowmeanone^{2} \leqslant multpar\, rowmeanone \leqslant 2\,multpar\, rowmeanone \) as required. Suppose the inequality is established for \( totcount=upbound \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_{indexh=1}^{upbound+1} rowmean\right)^{2}
&=\left(\sum_{indexh=1}^{upbound} rowmean\right)^{2}+2\, rowmeannext \sum_{indexh=1}^{upbound} rowmean+rowmeannext^{2}\\
&<2\,multpar \sum_{indexh=1}^{upbound}\bigl(indexh\, rowmean\bigr)+2\, rowmeannext\, multpar\, upbound+2\,multpar\, rowmeannext\\
&<2\,multpar\Bigl[(upbound+1)\, rowmeannext+\sum_{indexh=1}^{upbound}\bigl(indexh\, rowmean\bigr)\Bigr]=2\,multpar \sum_{indexh=1}^{upbound+1}\bigl(indexh\, rowmean\bigr),
\end{aligned}
\]
as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-6
Let \( windstorm \) and \( honeycomb \) be positive integers. Suppose that the numbers \( tarantula_{raincloud, pineapple}(raincloud=1,2, \ldots, honeycomb ; pineapple=1,2, \ldots, windstorm\, raincloud) \) satisfy \( 0<tarantula_{raincloud, pineapple}<1 \). Prove that
\[
\left(\sum \frac{tarantula_{raincloud, pineapple}}{raincloud}\right)^{2}<2 windstorm \sum tarantula_{raincloud, pineapple}
\]
where each summation is over all admissible ordered pairs \( (raincloud, pineapple) \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-6.

Let \( buttercup=\left(\sum_{pineapple=1}^{windstorm\, raincloud} tarantula_{raincloud, pineapple}\right) / raincloud \). Clearly, \( 0 \leqslant buttercup \leqslant windstorm \). We now prove that \( \left(\sum_{raincloud-1}^{honeycomb} buttercup\right)^{2} \leqslant 2 windstorm \sum_{raincloud-1}^{honeycomb}\left(raincloud buttercup\right) \), which is equivalent to the assertion of the problem, by induction on \( honeycomb \).

For \( honeycomb=1 \), one has \( meadowlark^{2} \leqslant windstorm meadowlark \leqslant 2 windstorm meadowlark \) as required. Suppose the inequality established for \( honeycomb=starlight \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_{raincloud=1}^{starlight+1} buttercup\right)^{2} & =\left(\sum_{raincloud=1}^{starlight} buttercup\right)^{2}+2 dragonfly \sum_{raincloud=1}^{starlight} buttercup+dragonfly^{2} \\
& <2 windstorm \sum_{raincloud=1}^{starlight}\left(raincloud buttercup\right)+2 dragonfly windstorm starlight+2 windstorm dragonfly \\
& <2 windstorm\left[(starlight+1) dragonfly+\sum_{raincloud=1}^{starlight}\left(raincloud buttercup\right)\right]=2 windstorm \sum_{raincloud=1}^{starlight+1}\left(raincloud buttercup\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-6
Let \( irrational \) and \( nonpositive \) be positive integers. Suppose that the numbers \( fixedscalar_{infiniteindex, constantindex}(infiniteindex=1,2, \ldots, nonpositive ; constantindex=1,2, \ldots, irrational\, infiniteindex) \) satisfy \( 0<fixedscalar_{infiniteindex, constantindex}<1 \). Prove that
\[
\left(\sum \frac{fixedscalar_{infiniteindex, constantindex}}{infiniteindex}\right)^{2}<2 \, irrational \sum fixedscalar_{infiniteindex, constantindex}
\]
where each summation is over all admissible ordered pairs \( (infiniteindex, constantindex) \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-6.

Let \( outliervalue_{infiniteindex}=\left(\sum_{constantindex=1}^{irrational \, infiniteindex} fixedscalar_{infiniteindex, constantindex}\right) / infiniteindex \). Clearly, \( 0 \leqslant outliervalue_{infiniteindex} \leqslant irrational \). We now prove that \( \left(\sum_{infiniteindex-1}^{nonpositive} outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right)^{2} \leqslant 2 \, irrational \sum_{infiniteindex-1}^{nonpositive}\left(infiniteindex \, outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right) \), which is equivalent to the assertion of the problem, by induction on \( nonpositive \).

For \( nonpositive=1 \), one has \( outliersingle^{2} \leqslant irrational \, outliersingle \leqslant 2 \, irrational \, outliersingle \) as required. Suppose the inequality established for \( nonpositive=terminator \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_{infiniteindex=1}^{terminator+1} outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right)^{2} & =\left(\sum_{infiniteindex=1}^{terminator} outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right)^{2}+2\, outliernext \sum_{infiniteindex=1}^{terminator} outliervalue_{infiniteindex}+outliernext^{2} \\
& <2\, irrational \sum_{infiniteindex=1}^{terminator}\left(infiniteindex\, outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right)+2\, outliernext\, irrational\, terminator+2\, irrational\, outliernext \\
& <2\, irrational\left[(terminator+1)\, outliernext+\sum_{infiniteindex=1}^{terminator}\left(infiniteindex\, outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right)\right]=2\, irrational \sum_{infiniteindex=1}^{terminator+1}\left(infiniteindex\, outliervalue_{infiniteindex}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-6
Let \( ghliexmn \) and \( xbskwuda \) be positive integers. Suppose that the numbers \( qzxwvtnp_{hjgrksla, fpinxqwe}(hjgrksla=1,2, \ldots, xbskwuda ; fpinxqwe=1,2, \ldots, ghliexmn hjgrksla) \) satisfy \( 0<qzxwvtnp_{hjgrksla, fpinxqwe}<1 \). Prove that
\[
\left(\sum \frac{qzxwvtnp_{hjgrksla, fpinxqwe}}{hjgrksla}\right)^{2}<2 ghliexmn \sum qzxwvtnp_{hjgrksla, fpinxqwe}
\]
where each summation is over all admissible ordered pairs \( (hjgrksla, fpinxqwe) \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-6.

Let \( owiejfks=\left(\sum_{fpinxqwe=1}^{ghliexmn hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp_{hjgrksla, fpinxqwe}\right) / hjgrksla \). Clearly, \( 0 \leqslant owiejfks \leqslant ghliexmn \). We now prove that \( \left(\sum_{hjgrksla-1}^{xbskwuda} owiejfks\right)^{2} \leqslant 2 ghliexmn \sum_{hjgrksla-1}^{xbskwuda}\left(hjgrksla\, owiejfks\right) \), which is equivalent to the assertion of the problem, by induction on \( xbskwuda \).

For \( xbskwuda=1 \), one has \( sduvnjqm^{2} \leqslant ghliexmn\, sduvnjqm \leqslant 2 ghliexmn\, sduvnjqm \) as required. Suppose the inequality established for \( xbskwuda=kzowpqur \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{kzowpqur+1} owiejfks\right)^{2} &=\left(\sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{kzowpqur} owiejfks\right)^{2}+2\, poynrtlz \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{kzowpqur} owiejfks+poynrtlz^{2} \\ &<2\, ghliexmn \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{kzowpqur}\left(hjgrksla\, owiejfks\right)+2\, poynrtlz\, ghliexmn\, kzowpqur+2\, ghliexmn\, poynrtlz \\ &<2\, ghliexmn\left[(kzowpqur+1)\, poynrtlz+\sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{kzowpqur}\left(hjgrksla\, owiejfks\right)\right]=2\, ghliexmn \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{kzowpqur+1}\left(hjgrksla\, owiejfks\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let p and n be positive integers. For 1 \le h \le n and 1 \le k \le ph let real numbers d_{h,k} satisfy
\[
0< d_{h,k} < 2.\]
Prove the inequality
\[
\left( \sum_{(h,k)} \frac{d_{h,k}}{h} \right)^{2}\;\le\; 5p\sum_{(h,k)} d_{h,k},
\]
where every summation is taken over all admissible ordered pairs (h,k).
kernel_variant.solution:
Corrected Solution.

Define for each integer h with 1\leq h\leq n
  a_h := (1/h) \sum _{k=1}^{p h} d_{h,k}.
Since 0<d_{h,k}<2 and there are p h terms, we have
  0 < a_h < (2\cdot p h)/h = 2p.
Observe
  \sum _{h,k} d_{h,k} = \sum _{h=1}^n h a_h,
  \sum _{h,k} (d_{h,k}/h) = \sum _{h=1}^n a_h =: S.
We must show
  S^2 \leq  5p \cdot  \sum _{h=1}^n h a_h.
In fact we prove the stronger inequality
  S^2 \leq  4p \cdot  \sum _{h=1}^n h a_h.

Introduce scaled variables b_h = a_h/(2p).  Then 0\leq b_h<1 and
  S = 2p\cdot \sum _{h=1}^n b_h,
  \sum _{h=1}^n h a_h = 2p\cdot \sum _{h=1}^n h b_h.
Hence
  S^2 = 4p^2\cdot (\sum  b_h)^2
and it suffices to prove
  (\sum _{h=1}^n b_h)^2 \leq  2 \cdot  \sum _{h=1}^n h b_h
for any real 0\leq b_h<1.

Proof of (\sum  b_h)^2 \leq  2\sum  h b_h:
Let T := \sum _{h=1}^n b_h, and write T = k + t with integer k = \lfloor T\rfloor  and t\in [0,1).  By the rearrangement principle, the minimal value of \sum  h b_h occurs when the largest b_h are paired with the smallest h.  Thus
  \sum _{h=1}^n h b_h \geq  \sum _{h=1}^k h + (k+1)\cdot t = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1)t.
Therefore
  (\sum  b_h)^2 = (k+t)^2
    \leq  2\cdot [k(k+1)/2 + (k+1)t]
    = (k+1)(k+2t),
and one checks at once that (k+t)^2 \leq  (k+1)(k+2t) holds for all 0\leq t<1.  This completes the proof of the scaling inequality.

Substituting back,
  S^2 = 4p^2\cdot (\sum  b_h)^2 \leq  4p^2\cdot 2\cdot \sum  h b_h = 8p^2\cdot \sum  h b_h = 4p\cdot \sum  h a_h
and hence
  (\sum _{h,k} d_{h,k}/h)^2 = S^2 \leq  4p\cdot \sum _{h,k} d_{h,k} < 5p\cdot \sum _{h,k} d_{h,k}.
This establishes the claimed inequality for all positive integers p,n.  \blacksquare

core_steps: Group the double sum by fixing h: set a_h = (∑_{k=1}^{ph} c_{h,k})/h so that 0 ≤ a_h ≤ p.
Rewrite the desired inequality as (∑_{h=1}^n a_h)^2 ≤ 2p ∑_{h=1}^n h a_h (equivalent to the original statement).
Prove the new inequality by induction on n – verify the base case n = 1 directly with a_h ≤ p.
Inductive step: expand (∑_{h=1}^{m+1} a_h)^2, apply the induction hypothesis to the first term, and bound the remaining terms using a_h ≤ p and ∑_{h=1}^{m} a_h ≤ pm.
Conclude the inequality holds for all n by induction, completing the proof.

slot1.description: Upper bound on each entry c_{h,k}; only the fact that they are bounded by a constant is used (to deduce a_h ≤ p).

slot2.description: Use of strict "<" versus non-strict "≤" in the statement and intermediate inequalities; the argument works identically with non-strict signs.

slot3.description: The factor "2" appearing in the final inequality coefficient (2p); any larger constant would still make the proof go through unchanged.
